content:
We are asked for the smallest prime \(p\) such that
\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k
\]
is not divisible by \(p\).

1.  Write the sum in closed‐form:
\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k
\;=\;\frac{9^{2011}-9}{9-1}
\;=\;\frac{9\bigl(9^{2010}-1\bigr)}{8}.
\]

2.  Check small primes in turn:

 •  \(p=2\):  Since \(9\equiv1\pmod8\), \(9^{2010}-1\equiv0\pmod8\), so the numerator is a multiple of \(8\cdot9\) and even.  Thus \(2\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=3\):  Every term \(9^k\equiv0\pmod3\), so \(3\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=5\):  \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), the powers of \(4\) mod 5 cycle of length 2 with sum \(4+1=5\equiv0\pmod5\), and \(2010\) is even, so \(5\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=7\):  \(9\equiv2\pmod7\), the powers of 2 mod 7 cycle of length 3 summing to \(2+4+1=7\equiv0\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 3, so \(7\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=11\):  \(9\pmod{11}\) has order 5 with cycle sum \(9+4+3+5+1=22\equiv0\pmod{11}\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 5, so \(11\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=13\):  \(9\pmod{13}\) has order 3 with sum \(9+3+1=13\equiv0\pmod{13}\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 3, so \(13\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=17\):  \(9\pmod{17}\) has order 8 with one–cycle sum
    \[
      9+13+15+16+8+4+2+1 =68\equiv0\pmod{17},
    \]
    but \(2010=8\cdot251+2\), so only the first \(2\) terms of the last (partial) cycle survive.  Those two terms are \(9^1+9^2\equiv9+13=22\equiv5\pmod{17}\neq0\).  Hence \(17\nmid S\).

All smaller primes \(2,3,5,7,11,13\) do divide \(S\), but \(17\) does not.  Therefore the answer is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

\[

1.  Write the sum in closed‐form:
\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k

 •  \(p=2\):  Since \(9\equiv1\pmod8\), \(9^{2010}-1\equiv0\pmod8\), so the numerator is a multiple of \(8\cdot9\) and even.  Thus \(2\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=3\):  Every term \(9^k\equiv0\pmod3\), so \(3\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=5\):  \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), the powers of \(4\) mod 5 cycle of length 2 with sum \(4+1=5\equiv0\pmod5\), and \(2010\) is even, so \(5\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=7\):  \(9\equiv2\pmod7\), the powers of 2 mod 7 cycle of length 3 summing to \(2+4+1=7\equiv0\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 3, so \(7\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=11\):  \(9\pmod{11}\) has order 5 with cycle sum \(9+4+3+5+1=22\equiv0\pmod{11}\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 5, so \(11\mid S\).  
 •  \(p=13\):  \(9\pmod{13}\) has order 3 with sum \(9+3+1=13\equiv0\pmod{13}\), and \(2010\) is a multiple of 3, so \(13\mid S\).
